drills.explanation: O-O was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Nxe4 ran into Qxe5+, costing about 4.1 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs bjjbutcher (495) — Four Knights Game
drills.coachNote: You're a full piece up here, and the one loose end is e5 sitting on the c3 queen's diagonal. Nxe4 attacks her but ignores that e5 falls with check: Qxe5+ forces Qe7, then Qxe7+ Kxe7 trades queens and the stranded e4 knight gets rounded up — your extra piece evaporates. O-O banks the win: king safe, rooks connected, and after Qe3 Qe7 c4 you stay a clean piece ahead.

drills.explanation: b3 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Kb7 ran into g6, costing about 3.8 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs bjjbutcher (495) — Four Knights Game
drills.coachNote: This is a pure race, and b3 wins it by deflection: Rxa4 takes your rook, but b2 promotes anyway — the rook can't get back, so after Kh5 b1=Q Rxa5 it's your queen against rook and g-pawn, and only you are playing for a win. Kb7 helps neither pawn, and g6 uses the gift: now the same sequence ends with White queening too — queen plus rook against your lone queen.

drills.title: vs sheresingh (492) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Black's king is trapped on f5 in the open — this is a mating position, not a material-counting one. Rxe5+ gives the exchange back to drag it forward: Kxe5 Re1+ Kf6 Qd7 and the net closes. Qb6 drifts to the queenside and buys Black Rhe8 to contest the e-file; you still had to find Rxe5+ a move later anyway, with half of +19.9 gone.

drills.coachNote: Black's queen just stepped onto d6 — directly on your f4 bishop's diagonal, with exd6 the only recapture. Bxd6 wins queen for bishop on the spot; that is the entire +6.7. Nb3 gave her a free exit with Qd7, and after a4 e6 you were back to a normal game having declined a whole queen.

drills.title: vs tdwu (493) — Indian Game East Indian London System...4.e3 O O
drills.coachNote: The d8-e7-f6 diagonal is wide open, so f6 belongs to Black's queen — Qf6 isn't an attack, it's a queen left en prise: Qxf6 and nothing recaptures. With Black's knight already dug into g3 beside your king, that turns +3.7 into a lost game, and Ne2+ tricks follow immediately. Rfe1 was the move: challenge the e5 rook, hold the e-file, and meet Nf5 with f4.

drills.title: vs tdwu (493) — Indian Game East Indian London System...4.e3 O O
drills.coachNote: f3 kicks the e4 knight somewhere better and gives up e3's only defender — the f2 pawn. Ng5 comes, and Rxe3 follows because nothing guards that square anymore. Meanwhile the a5 pawn was simply free: Rxa5, and after Re8 Rxd5 you're harvesting the queenside two pawns up while the knight has no targets left.

drills.title: vs Homner_Sama (485) — Englund Gambit Mosquito Gambit
drills.coachNote: Count the attackers on f2: Black's queen from c5 and the knight on g4, versus your king alone. Be2 ignores it, and Qxf2+ crashes in — Kd2 is forced and your king starts walking while Nc6 brings reinforcements. Nd4 closes the c5-f2 diagonal, and after a5 f3 the g4 knight gets pushed back with your advantage intact.

drills.title: vs Krishna_Sharma8897 (514) — Slav Defense
drills.coachNote: White's center holds by a thread: Nxe5 works immediately, because after fxe5, Bf5 hits the e4 queen and the d3 bishop on one diagonal — the queen steps away and Qxd3 wins the bishop, with White's king still stuck in the middle. Rb8 spends the one tempo that mattered: O-O follows and the shot is gone — cxb4 Bc4 and it's White's kingside pawns doing the attacking. You were +2.3 with exactly one chance; this was it.

drills.explanation: Qh4 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, fxg6 left that piece hanging: after Qxg6+ it's simply lost.
drills.title: vs Krishna_Sharma8897 (514) — Slav Defense
drills.coachNote: The g6 bishop is bait: White's queen on c2 stands behind it on the b1-h7 diagonal, so fxg6 opens your own king — Qxg6+ Kh8 Qxh6+, and with the b2 bishop and the e5 pawn trained on g7, the attack runs itself. You were worse but still fighting; this made it immediate. Qh4 was the chance — it hits f4 and h3 and forces White to answer you, and after Bf5 cxb4 you have genuine counterplay.

drills.explanation: Nd8 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Nf4 ran into d7, costing about 14.6 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs trinangkurchatterjee (836) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Variation
drills.coachNote: Your e6 knight has one job: blockading the d6 passer. Nf4 hits the d5 rook, but White doesn't blink — d7! and the pawn is through: Re1 gets removed by Nxe1 and d8=Q follows. Nd8 was ugly and correct — the knight sits on the queening square itself, and after Ne5 Rc2 Rxb5 Rd2 you're still resisting. Down as you were, the blockade was the whole game.

drills.explanation: b6 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, f5 ran into Rxe6, costing about 3.2 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs trinangkurchatterjee (836) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Variation
drills.coachNote: f5 attacks the e4 rook but abandons f7's guard of e6 — Rxe6 just takes, the rook is safe there, and your c8 bishop stays buried behind the wreckage. After Nf6 Re2 you're a pawn down with the worse structure. b6 was what the position wanted: Bb7 comes next, the bishop finally breathes, and only then does the centralized rook — which has no permanent home — become a real target.

drills.title: vs Renzo883 (494) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Your d2 knight is already hanging to the b2 queen — nothing defends it. Rab1 attacks her, but she doesn't have to move: Qxd2 eats the knight, and Rfd1 Qxc3 leaves her a piece and two pawns to the good, perfectly safe. Nxd7 was the counterpunch: it trades off first, and Qxd5 then wins the central pawn with threats of its own — activity instead of a free knight for Black.

drills.title: vs Renzo883 (494) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Two loose points define this position: your b2 pawn under the b6 queen, and Black's d7 bishop, which your e5 knight can take. h3 addresses neither — Qxb2 lands, and the queen sits in your camp leaning on d2 and c3. Nxd7 Nxd7 Qxd5 was the sequence: you win the d5 pawn and attack the new d7 knight, and after Nf6 Qd4 you're offering the queen trade from strength.

drills.title: vs Masterpeaces (490) — Birds Opening
drills.coachNote: White's king on f1 has no pawn cover, your rook already sits on g3, and the h4 knight covers f3 and g2 — Rxe3 takes the last shelter pawn for free and threatens Rf3+ next. Qh1+ is the check that helps White: Ke2 walks toward safety and your queen is stranded in the corner, far from the hunt, reducing you to grinding with cxd3+ and Qxh3 instead of finishing.

drills.coachNote: White's d2 pawn is indefensible: your queen and d8 rook are doubled on the file, so Qxd2 wins it — recapturing runs into Rxd2 hitting the c2 bishop, so White must grovel with Rf2, when Qxe3 takes a second pawn. Qd5 centralizes but threatens nothing: Rf2 holds, and after c3 dxc3 the d2 target is gone. The battery was already built; it just needed firing.

drills.title: vs asombro1 (506) — Englund Gambit
drills.coachNote: The e5 pawn covers d4 and f4 — Bf4 develops the bishop onto a square a pawn already attacks, and exf4 wins it outright on move two. The Englund's entire hope is that you won't simply take: dxe5 accepts the pawn, and after Nc6 Nf3 d5 you develop normally, a healthy pawn up with zero compensation for Black.

drills.title: vs asombro1 (506) — Englund Gambit
drills.coachNote: Your g3 queen is attacked by the c7 bishop, but you owned the stronger threat: Bxf6 hits the d8 queen, so Bxg3 runs into Bxd8, and Bxf2+ Kxf2 Kxd8 leaves you a piece for a pawn ahead. Qe3+ retreats with a check that costs Black nothing — the g4 bishop drops back to e6, blocking while improving itself, and after Nf3 Nbd7 O-O Black has caught up and your edge is gone.

drills.explanation: Qd5 forks the queen on d3 and the rook on b7 — one of them falls next move. In the game, a2 ran into Rb8+, costing about 14.5 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs C4RL05707 (835) — Queens Pawn Opening Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Your king on d8 stands on White's promotion square with the rook poised on b7: Rb8+ Ke7 d8=Q+ wins your queen — Qxd8 Rxd8 and it's resignable. a2 is exactly one move too slow to that. Qd5 was the defense precisely because it forces queens off: Qxd5 exd5 disarms the b8/d8 combination, and then e4 and Bc5 activate while your a-pawn stays a real trump. Losing, yes — but this move ended the game.

drills.explanation: Bb5+ forks the king on e2 and the king on e2 — one of them falls next move. In the game, Qc4+ ran into Qd3, costing about 6.6 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs C4RL05707 (835) — Queens Pawn Opening Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Move order was everything: Qc4+ lets White block with Qd3, and the pin Bb5 gets answered by Qxc4 Bxc4+ — queens off, your +5 shrunk to technique. Bb5+ first flips it: the d7 bishop arrives with check, Ke1 is forced, and Qxa1+ wins the corner rook outright — after Qd1 Qxd1+ Kxd1 you're simply a rook up. Same two pieces, reversed order, a full rook's difference.

drills.title: vs penaldoqq (800) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: The checking rook on f8 is alone — Ke7 attacks it, and its only trick, Rf7+, drops it to Kxf7. That ends the game. Be8 blocks instead and pins your own bishop to the king: after g3 you spend moves on Kd7 and c5 untangling while the rook holds your bishop hostage and your extra queen watches. You still won, but a one-move finish became a project.

drills.explanation: Bf5 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Qxc2 ran into Qe5+, costing about 8.0 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs penaldoqq (800) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: Your b2 queen has a quiet job: along the b2-e5 diagonal she's the only cover on e5, where White's queen wants to check, forking your uncastled king and the loose h8 rook. Qxc2 takes a pawn and abandons the post — Qe5+ Kd8 Qxh8+ follows at once. Bf5 keeps the diagonal guarded while developing, and the follow-up shows the idea: after g4, castle anyway — gxf5 Rae8 and your rooks flood in against White's ripped-open king.

drills.explanation: Rh7 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Rh4 ran into Ra3+, costing about 8.8 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs sudhanshu_chessing (500) — Indian Game...3.e3 d5
drills.coachNote: Your rook had to move, but Rh4 leaves it one square from the enemy king with only your own king guarding it — and a guard can be deflected: Ra3+ forces Kg2, and Kxh4 takes the rook for free, the move that lost this game. Rh7 keeps distance: it leans on g7, your knight and pawns hold, and the checks dry up after Kf2 Ra2+ Kf3 — Black has nothing left while your +4 converts.

drills.coachNote: The e5 knight had a family check: Nxg6+ is untouchable — Black's h-pawn sits on h6, not h7, so nothing recaptures — and after Ke8, Nxf4 wins the rook and the game on the spot. Rxh6 pockets a pawn and lets the rook live: Rxa4 takes yours back, and after Rxg6 c5 the c-pawn starts running with the rook behind it — the counterplay that eventually cost you the game.

drills.title: vs pittybow (498) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: The seventh rank is only gold when the rook is safe there. Ra7 walks into Qb6 and the rook has no exit: Rxa6 loses to Qxa6, Rxc7 to Qxc7, b7 is covered by the queen, and a8 by the c8 rook — trapped. Qa4 attacks a6 the safe way, and the point is elegant: after Rb8, the very same Ra7 works, because now the queen on a4 guards it.

drills.coachNote: Trace the e4 bishop's line: d3 and c2 are empty, so it points straight at b1 — the exact square you chose. Bxb1 wins the exchange, and Qxb1 Qxb1 Rxb1 trades into an ending where you're exchange-down in a position that was already worse. Nd7 was the resource: it hits the f8 rook, then Nc5 harasses the e4 bishop, and Qb3 offers the queen trade on your terms, untangling everything.

drills.coachNote: The d4/e5 tension won't wait for b3: Black captures first with exd4, and after Qxd4 the e7 knight lands on d5, hitting your queen and c3 with tempo — the +2.5 is gone in two moves. dxe5 resolves it your way: after dxe5, Nf3 comes for the pawn, and neither black knight — parked on b6 and e7 — can defend it in time; Nxe5 and you're a clean pawn up.

drills.coachNote: Black's d6 pawn is free right now: the d7 knight blocks the queen's defense and nothing else touches it, so Bxd6 simply wins it and plants your bishop in the heart of Black's camp. Nbd2 gave Black time to fix everything with tempo — e5!, and after dxe5 dxe5 your f4 bishop gets shoved back to h2 while Black claims the center.

drills.coachNote: You had a forced finish: Rxf2+ Rxf2 Qxf2+ Kh3 Qg2+ and the white king gets run down with your queen, rook and bishop all in the hunt. Rxg3 instead trusts the e3 queen to guard the rook — but Nxe4! wins your bishop and adds a second attacker on g3, so after Qxe4 the guard is gone and Kxg3 collects. From +19.9, this one capture handed back the material and lost the game.

drills.explanation: Qe1+ forks the king on g3 and the king on g3 — one of them falls next move. In the game, Qg1+ ran into Kf4, costing about 19.3 pawns of advantage.

drills.coachNote: White's knight on e5 is loose and the queen is far away on c8, so the checking path decides everything: Qe1+ Kg4 Qe2+ Kg3 Qe3+ climbs the e-file with checks until your queen attacks the e5 knight directly — White never gets a breath. Qg1+ opens the wrong door: Kf4! and the king escapes to the center beside its knight; the checks fizzle, White regroups with Nd7, and a completely winning position slipped into a loss.

drills.coachNote: With queens facing off on the e-file, every knight move is a zwischenzug risk: Nf4 attacks the e2 queen, but White trades first — Qxe7+ Bxe7 — and then Bxf4 scoops the knight, which the c1 bishop had covered all along. A clearly better position became a piece-down one in one move. Be6 keeps the d5 knight anchored and develops: after Qe4 you castle long, hit the queen with Nb4, and your development lead starts to matter.

Bxh1 swapped it for the rook, and after Kxh1 you had to grind out Rd2 and Rxa2 in a position that was mate in two. Rd1+ forces Kh2 (g3 and h3 are White's own pawns), and Rxh1 is mate because the bishop covers g2.

drills.coachNote: Your e7-bishop was guarded only by the king on e8, so castling walked away from it — White's queen on e2 sees e7 straight down the open file. Qxe7 took the piece, and Rfe8 plus Bxf3 never got full value back. Qd7 first keeps the bishop covered and prepares to castle a move later; after Be5 O-O h3 Bh5 the position stays roughly level.

This was the move that turned a winning endgame into the loss. After Rxe4 Kf7 f4, the extra material decides.

Rc2 chased the c3-bishop instead and let Black liquidate: Rxe2, Rxe2, Rb2 and everything comes off, dissolving a +7 position into near-equality in a game you went on to lose. After Rxe7+ Kh8 Rde2, the doubled rooks end it quickly.

drills.title: vs CeliaDuchesne (819) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Everything ran through Black's d2 pawn, and your g4 bishop was its only jailer — the one piece covering d1. Worse, that bishop was takeable with check: Ra7 allowed Rxg4+, and after Kh2 d1=Q the new queen ended it — this single move threw away a winning position and the game. Rd7 puts the rook behind the passer where it belongs: Rxd2 follows within a few moves and your extra kingside pawns decide.

drills.explanation: Rad1 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Re2 ran into Nf3+, costing about 3.5 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs CeliaDuchesne (819) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Black's e5 knight had a family check brewing on f3, hitting your g1 king and h4 queen at once — and Re2 put the rook exactly where the follow-up cashes in. Nf3+ forces Nxf3 (gxf3 drops the rook the same way), then Rxe2 wins rook for knight, and Nd4 kicking the rook doesn't get it back. Rad1 keeps the rooks connected and out of reach; after Bf5 you have Ne4, trading the attackers and keeping your +2.

drills.explanation: bxc6 simply wins the bishop on c6 — it wasn't adequately defended. In the game, Bd7 left that piece hanging: after Bxd7+ it's simply lost.
drills.title: vs Dgreek46 (678) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: White's bishop had just captured on c6 and stood on a square your b7 pawn covers — bxc6 takes it back and material is dead level. Bd7 declined the recapture and offered a trade instead: Bxd7+ Qxd7 and the piece is gone for good, White a clean knight up out of the opening. After bxc6 Bf4 f6, the doubled pawns are a small price — Ne7 and Re1 later, you're simply equal.

drills.explanation: Qxd4+ forks the king on g1 and the king on g1 — one of them falls next move. In the game, Nxd2 left that piece hanging: after Qxd2 it's simply lost.
drills.title: vs Dgreek46 (678) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: Two captures were on the table and only one order works. Qxd4+ takes the pawn with check — Kh1 is forced, no time to save anything — and then Nxd2 follows, netting both. Taking on d2 first let Qxd2 recapture, and from d2 the queen defends d4, so the extra pawn vanished. Down as much as you were, winning material with tempo was the only road back.

drills.explanation: Qc7# is checkmate on the spot — the king has no escape squares. In the game, Qb7+ ran into Kd8, costing about 9.2 pawns of advantage.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs aksu10ten (599) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Your h2 bishop quietly covers c7 all the way down the b8-h2 diagonal — g3, f4, e5 and d6 are empty — which makes Qc7 checkmate on the spot: the king can't capture a defended queen, and b8 and d8 are covered. Qb7+ let the king slide out to d8, and after Qxc6 Black even got Qxg4+ throwing checks. You still won, but mate-in-one became several more moves of care.

drills.explanation: Qd7 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Qd3 ran into f5, costing about 7.8 pawns of advantage.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs aksu10ten (599) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: From b5 your queen had Qd7, crashing onto the seventh rank through the empty c6 square — even Black's best, Qxf2+ Rxf2, only delays it, because once the e-pawn moves, Qxh7 wins the rook sitting there. Qd3 aims at h7 too, but across the open board, where f5 slams the diagonal shut and gnaws at g4. Most of your +10 evaporated in one move: the rank was the road the pawns couldn't block.

drills.explanation: Rd1 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Re5 ran into Qxc1+, costing about 3.1 pawns of advantage.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs Sadrios (652) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Your c1 rook was hanging to the a3 queen along the a3-c1 diagonal — b2 is empty — and nothing defended it. That rook had to move; instead the other one jumped to e5, attacking the e6 rook but forcing nothing. Qxc1+ took a free rook with check, and after Kh2 Rg6 Black consolidated. Rd1 saves it, and after c5 Qf3 Qc3 your queen heads for d5 with genuine chances to hold despite being worse.

drills.explanation: c4 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, dxe5 left that piece hanging: after Nxe5 it's simply lost.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs Sadrios (652) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Your whole edge lived in one sequence: c4 kicks the d5 queen, and after Qd7, Nxe4 scoops the loose straggler on e4 — that's the +2. dxe5 released the center instead: Nxe5 recaptures, and the same c4 idea now fails because the e5 knight answers Nxc4 itself. One pawn trade and both targets — the exposed queen and the e4 pawn — stopped being targets; the position was dead equal.

drills.explanation: Qd2 forks the rook on e1 and the knight on h6 — one of them falls next move. In the game, Qb6 ran into Nxf7+, costing about 3.2 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs Ntk2711 (850) — Caro Kann Defense Karpov Variation
drills.coachNote: White's h6 knight and c4 bishop form a battery against f7 — the bishop backs the knight, so your f8 rook alone can't hold the point. Qb6 drifted away from the kingside and Nxf7+ landed, forking toward h8; after Kg7 Rf3 you had to bail out with Qg6 and most of your +4.5 was gone. Qd2 attacks the h6 knight down the d2-h6 diagonal — it must hop to g4 — and then Qxc2 starts collecting, with Bxf7 met by Bxb3.

drills.explanation: Qh5 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Qd4 ran into Nxf7+, costing about 1.4 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs Ntk2711 (850) — Caro Kann Defense Karpov Variation
drills.coachNote: Your king on h8 has White's knight parked right beside it on h6, and f7 is the square holding your kingside together. Qd4 looks active but drops exactly that: Nxf7+, and after Rxf7 Rxf7 White has traded the knight for rook and pawn, clawing back most of your +4.5. Qh5 attacks the stranded h6 knight instead — after Nf5 you hit the d3 bishop with c4, and when the knight lands on g3 your queen slides to h6, keeping every threat alive.

drills.explanation: a5 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Ng6 left that piece hanging: after Nxg6 it's simply lost.
drills.title: vs FlokieXIV (796) — Caro Kann Defense Advance Variation
drills.coachNote: White's a4-b4 pawns are the real threat — your b6 bishop is running out of air. Ng6 volunteers a trade that only helps White: Nxg6 fxg6 wrecks your kingside cover, and a5 hits the bishop anyway, forcing the sorry retreat to c7. a5 yourself was the move: it fixes b4 in place, and after b5 you strike back with c5 against d4, giving the b6 bishop scope and your queen the c7 square with real counterplay.

drills.explanation: Ne5 keeps the attack on the stranded king on f6 alive: the threat is Nd7+, and after Qg8, Nd7+ Kg5 h4+ drags the king even further up the board while your queen sits on h7. Qxb7 won a bishop but allowed Qd5 — a queen trade you can't decline (Qxd5 exd5) — and with queens off, the attack on f6 vanished, about five pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs sudharsanee (850) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Variation
drills.coachNote: You're +8 because the black king is stranded on f6 — the queens must stay on. Qxb7 grabs a bishop but steps onto the d5-b7 diagonal: Qd5! forces the trade, and after Qxd5 exd5 the attack is gone and the recapture even tidies Black's structure. Ne5 keeps hunting: it threatens Nd7+, and in the line Qg8 Nd7+ Kg5 h4+ the king gets dragged up the board into the open where your pieces finish the job.

drills.explanation: Ne5 puts the knight on a square the d4 pawn protects and attacks the knight on g6. If Nxe5, dxe5+ regains the piece with check, and after Qxe5 your Qf3+ keeps queens on against his airy king. Your Qf3+ instead offered the trade Black wanted: Qxf3 gxf3 killed all the pressure, and Nf4 came next, poking at the h3 pawn.
drills.title: vs sudharsanee (850) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Variation
drills.coachNote: Black's queen on d5 covers f3 through e4, so Qf3+ is really a queen-trade offer — exactly what the defender wants. Qxf3 gxf3 shreds your kingside, and Nf4 jumps into the new hole hitting h3; still winning, but +8 became a grind. Ne5 first is the right order: Nxe5 dxe5+ comes with check and deflects the black queen to e5, and only then Qf3+ — now completely safe — drives the king back with the attack rolling.

drills.explanation: Both your queen and rook could capture the knight on f4, but Qxf4 is the right way: it wins the knight while the rook on f8 keeps guarding its partner on e8. Rxf4 abandoned that rook — Qxe8+ snapped it off with check, and after Kh7, Qe6 left White completely consolidated. Taking with the wrong piece cost about six pawns.
drills.title: vs prusten63 (873) — Italian Game
drills.coachNote: Your f8 rook has one job: guarding e8, which White's queen on h5 hits straight through g6 and f7. Rxf4 abandons that post — Qxe8+ wins a whole rook, and after Kh7 Qe6 White consolidated; this is the move that lost the game. Qxf4 was the right capture: the queen takes the knight, steps out of the h5 queen's attack, and leaves f8 defending e8 — after Qe2 you even get e3, rolling the passed pawn.

drills.explanation: a6 strikes at White's overextended queenside: after bxa6 Rxa6 your rook enters the game down the a-file, and b5 is met calmly by Ra8. Castling let White play Qe2, hitting your knight on c4 — a piece with no defenders — and you had to scramble with c6 and b5 just to hold it, leaving White a wedge pawn on c6.
drills.title: vs prusten63 (873) — Italian Game
drills.coachNote: Your c4 knight has no retreat: b4 covers a5, the b1 knight covers a3, the c3 bishop covers b2 and d2, f2 covers e3, and b6, d6 and e5 are your own pawns. Castling hands White the free hit Qe2 against the trapped piece, and the rescue try c6 bxc6 b5 loses material around it. a6 was urgent: pry the queenside open first — bxa6 Rxa6, and when b5 comes the rook returns to a8 having loosened White's bind.

drills.explanation: You were a full piece up and just needed to consolidate. c5 gains space, and the knight calmly reroutes to c6 next with b6 and normal development to follow. Ng4 put the knight on a square covered by White's queen all the way back on d1 — Qxg4 took it for nothing, handing the extra piece straight back.
drills.opening: Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.title: vs Feri206 (798) — Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.coachNote: From d1, White's queen looks straight down the d1-g4 diagonal at the square your knight just picked. Qxg4 wins the piece on the spot; the e5 break afterward never came close to trapping her, and Qg3 kept everything. c5 was the plan: stake out the queenside, meet Re1 with Nc6, and after b4 b6 Bb2 you're fully developed in a level middlegame instead of a knight down.

drills.explanation: Bxa2 trades off White's light-squared bishop on your terms: after Rxa2 you swing rooks to the e-file with Rae8 and Re6, sitting solidly behind the e5 pawn. Rad8 left the bishops eyeballing each other one move too long — Bh6 came with threats at your king, and after g6, Bxe6 fxe6 left you with doubled, weak e-pawns.
drills.opening: Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.title: vs Feri206 (798) — Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.coachNote: Down four pawns of eval, the a2-e6 bishop standoff was your lifeline: Bxa2 removes White's most dangerous attacker, and after Rxa2 Rae8 your rooks stay active enough to resist. Rad8 leaves both enemy bishops on the board, and they pile in immediately: Bh6 forces g6, then Bxe6 fxe6 strips your king and shreds the pawns. The trade you skipped was the difference between a hard defense and a collapsing one.

drills.explanation: Nxc6 wins a full rook: the knight captures its counterpart and forks the queen on e7 and the rook on d8, so after Qd6 saves the queen, Nxd8 collects the rook and Qxg5 even adds a pawn. a4 instead gave Black time for Nxe5, trading off your monster knight, and after Qxe5 his other knight landed on e4 with real counterplay.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs TheArtifier (833) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Nxc6 wins material right now: from c6 the knight forks the e7 queen and d8 rook, and bxc6 instead would rip open the c8 king's cover — the line Qd6 Nxd8 Qxd8 Qxg5 leaves you an exchange and a pawn up. a4 spends the one tempo that mattered, and Black bails out with Nxe5: after Qxe5, Ne4 lands hitting c5 and f2, f3 is forced, and your +4.8 dissolved into the mess that eventually cost the game.

drills.explanation: Nxc6 is a fork: from c6 the knight hits both the queen on e7 and the rook on d8, so bxc6 is forced — wrecking the pawn cover in front of the king on c8. Then Qd3 turns on the knight on e4, and after Nxc5 bxc5 you're clearly on top. b5 abandoned the b4 pawn's guard of your c5 knight, and Nxe5 won material — no recapture worked.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs TheArtifier (833) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: b5 removes the b4 pawn — one of only two defenders of your c5 knight — and Black pounces with Nxe5: if dxe5 recaptures, the d4 pawn, the other defender, disappears too, and Qxc5 slides down from e7 to collect the piece. In the game bxa6 bxa6 followed and your grip dissolved. Nxc6 first was still right: bxc6 Qd3, and after Nxc5 bxc5 the queenside pawns keep cramping Black while you hold the edge.

drills.explanation: Rxf7 forces mate in three: your queen on d5 X-rays g8 so f7 can't really be held, and after Rg7+ Kh8, Rg8 is checkmate — the bishop on f6 seals g7 while the queen on d5 guards g8, so the rook can't be taken. Qg5 instead let Black play Rxf2 and keep the counterplay alive.
drills.title: vs yassine3453 (860) — Englund Gambit
drills.coachNote: You had forced mate: Rxf7! clears f7 so your d5 queen covers g8 along the diagonal, and Rg7+ Kh8 Rg8# finishes, with the f6 bishop sealing g7 the whole way. Qg5 walks away from all of it, and Black's second-rank battery erupts: Rxf2! with the queen on b2 behind it turns you into the defender, forced into Qe5 and grovelling. Twenty pawns of advantage — and the game — gone in one move.

drills.explanation: Qxc8 wins the rook on c8 outright — Black never dares recapture, since your bishop on f6 and rook on e7 strangle his king; he tries Qb5, and after Re8 he must throw his queen away with Qf1+ Rxf1. Qd5 let a won game slip: Qxa3, and after Qxd4, Qxh3 stripped your king — nearly fifteen pawns of advantage gone.
drills.title: vs yassine3453 (860) — Englund Gambit
drills.coachNote: Black's c8 rook is simply hanging to your c4 queen — the c-file between them is empty. Qxc8 wins a whole rook, and the desperado Qf1+ at the end of the line is met by Rxf1. Qd5 walks past it and invites Black's queen to feast: Qxa3, and while you grabbed d4 she took h3 as well, forcing R7e3 just to shield your king. You still won, but fifteen pawns of evaluation left in a single move.

drills.explanation: The g5 pawn was attacking your bishop on f4, and Nxg5 meets that head-on — the knight is covered by that same bishop, and after fxg5 Bxg5 Black's kingside pawns are stripped while his pieces retreat to passive squares like Nf6 and Nb8 as Qf3 builds pressure. Bf5+ dropped a whole piece instead: e6 blocked the check and hit the bishop, and after Bc2, gxf4 won your dark-squared bishop.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs jatin221272 (817) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: The real problem is your f4 bishop, attacked by g5 — and this is the second time you've tried this same check. e6! blocks and gains another tempo on the f5 bishop; after Bc2, gxf4 lands and Black untangles with the better game. Nxg5 met the threat head-on: fxg5 Bxg5 and your pieces swarm the king stuck on d7 — Nf6 is answered by Qf3, and Black's c6 knight ends up crawling home to b8.

drills.explanation: Nxg5 was the shot: after fxg5 Bxg5 you get two pawns for the knight and Black's king on d7 is stranded, with Qf3 coming to hit the loose d5 pawn (the simple e4 was strong too). Bf5+ backfired — e6 blocked with tempo, the bishop crawled back to c2, and gxf4 scooped up your dark-squared bishop, which the g5 pawn had been eyeing all along.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs jatin221272 (817) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Your f4 bishop is hanging to the g5 pawn, and Bf5+ ignores it — e6 blocks the check, hits your bishop, and after Bc2 Black plays gxf4 having gained two tempi. e4! was the punch: with the black king on d7, opening the center matters more than the bishop — after gxf4, exd5 hits the c6 knight, and when it retreats to a7, Qa4+ arrives with a raging attack on the exposed king.

drills.explanation: Your knight on e4 was attacked by the d3 pawn, and Nd6 is the safe retreat — White wins the e5 pawn with Rxe5+, but Be7 blocks, Bg6 follows, and you're only down a pawn with pieces coming out. Ng5 ran the knight straight onto the c1-bishop's diagonal: Bxg5 won it outright, and White grabbed e5 with the knight as well.
drills.title: vs Stwilso1 (836) — Ruy Lopez Opening Berlin Defense
drills.coachNote: Your e4 knight was hit by d3, but g5 is worse: the c1-g5 diagonal is wide open and the f3 knight covers g5 too — two attackers against your queen's lone defense. Bxg5 wins a piece, since Qxg5 runs into Nxg5; after the game's f6, Nxe5 grabbed your center pawn as well. Nd6 was the honest move: Rxe5+ Be7 concedes a pawn, but after Qe2 Bg6 Bf4 you're developed, solid, and still in the game.

drills.explanation: Your knight on g5 was attacked by the bishop on c1, defended only by your queen along the d8–g5 diagonal — and Be7 blocked that exact diagonal. Bxg5 won the knight, and after Be6, Bxe7 Qxe7 left White a clean piece up. Ne6 just steps to safety; after Qf3 Qf6 Nxc6 Bxd3 the position stays a fair fight.
drills.title: vs Stwilso1 (836) — Ruy Lopez Opening Berlin Defense
drills.coachNote: Be7 does defend g5, but the defense is fake: Bxg5 works anyway, because recapturing Bxg5 runs into Qh5!, hitting the bishop and f7 at once with the e5 knight backing the threats. So the game went Be6 Bxe7 Qxe7 and you were simply a piece down. Ne6 saved the knight outright — step it out of the bishop's reach, and after Qf3 Qf6 Nxc6 you have Bxd3 in return, keeping the damage contained.

drills.explanation: Bc7 hits the queen on d8 and grabs a square deep in Black's camp: after Qd7, b6 locks the bishop on c7 permanently, cramping the whole queenside while Ra4 swings the rook toward the action. Your e4 gave Black a free move for Bd7, developing with a hit on the b5 pawn — the same Bc7 idea then came a tempo late, and the queen slipped away to c8.
drills.title: vs slammerman001 (850) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Bc7 was sitting in your position: the f4 bishop hits the d8 queen, and after Qd7, b6 clamps the queenside — Nc6 is met by Ra4 and every black piece ends up tied down. e4 releases the center at the wrong moment and hands Black his best move for free: Bd7! hits the b5 pawn, and by the time you got Bc7 and b6 in anyway, the bishop was active and the eval had swung two-plus pawns against you.

drills.explanation: Qc5 does three jobs: it confronts the queen on b6, attacks the knight on e7, and keeps the b5 pawn defended. After Qd8, Bd6 piles onto e7, and Bxe7 Rxe7 leaves you in full control of the dark squares. Qc3 left b5 with no defender at all — Bd7 came immediately, and after your too-late Qc5, Bxb5 took the pawn for free.
drills.title: vs slammerman001 (850) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: The threat here was Black's, not yours: Bd7 is coming against b5, which nothing defends — and Qc3 does nothing to meet it. In the game Bd7 arrived, and after Qc5 Bxb5 the pawn and your queenside pressure were gone. Qc5 one move sooner changes the picture: it confronts the b6 queen directly, and on Qd8 you get Bd6, planting the bishop — Re8 Bxe7 Rxe7 and you keep a pleasant grip instead of drifting.

drills.explanation: Nf5+ is a fork: it checks the king on g3 and attacks the rook on g7 at the same time. After Kf4 you play Nxg7, winning the rook and flipping the game — roughly twelve pawns of swing. Nb7 instead walked into Rc7+, and after Kd8 the rook simply captured the knight on b7.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs OdisseoAP (826) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: You had a saving fork: Nf5+! hits the g3 king and the g7 rook at once, and after Kf4 Nxg7 you've won a whole rook — from four pawns down to right back in the game. Nb7 puts the knight on the one square where White plays the same idea in reverse: Rc7+ forks king and knight, Kd8 Rxb7, and it's over. The fork geometry was on the board — it was pointing your way first.

drills.explanation: Bxg3+ wins a pawn with check, and after the forced Kxg3, Rxd1 scoops the rook — d1 had no defender at all, so you net rook and pawn for the bishop. Rd7 put your rook on a square nothing defends (the knight on c6 doesn't cover d7): Rxd7 took it for free, and after Bd6, bxc6 grabbed your knight as well.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs OdisseoAP (826) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: At +6 this was winning easily, but nothing of yours covers d7 — Rd7 hangs the rook to a plain Rxd7, and that one square flipped a won game into the loss. The clean order was Bxg3+! first: your attacked bishop leaves the g3 pawn's bite with check, and after Kxg3 you play Rxd1, netting a whole rook. Even the immediate Rxd1 trade kept you winning; the rook just could not stop on d7.

drills.explanation: Qxb7+ forces checkmate: the queen grabs b7 and checks the king on e7 along the wide-open seventh rank, Black must block with Qc7, and Qxc7 is mate — your knight on f6 covers the escape squares e8 and d7, and the e5 pawn guards the knight. exd4 was a pawn grab with mate on the board; after Bg7 Black started breathing again.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs pisitto999 (814) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Qxb7+ was forced mate: the f3-b7 diagonal is open, the check runs along the seventh to the king on e7, Qc7 is the only block, and Qxc7 is mate with your f6 knight covering e8. exd4 wins a pawn instead of the game and gives Black air — Bg7 leans on the f6 knight, and after dxc5 Qc7 you needed Rd7+ to keep control. You converted anyway, but +20 with the king trapped mid-board was a two-move finish.

drills.explanation: Rd7+ is a deflection: the queen on c7 has to capture, and Nxd7 then forks the rook on b8 and the bishop on f8 — you've won queen for rook, with Rd1 coming to support the knight. Nd7 tried the fork without the preparation: Bg7 simply stepped the bishop out, nothing was won, and Rd6 got met by Rbc8. The open d-file was the key — use it with check first.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs pisitto999 (814) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Rd7+! wins the queen: the check on the e7 king forces Qxd7, your f6 knight recaptures, and from d7 it forks the b8 rook and f8 bishop on the way out — queen for rook. Nd7 goes to the same square without the deflection, so Black simply plays Bg7 and saves the b8 rook with Rbc8, leaving you to grind with Rd6 and Rad1 — most of your +8 intact but the knockout missed.

drills.explanation: Your queen on a4 was attacked by the knight on c5 and had to reach safety first — Qa3 does it. The Qh4 attacking idea was right but needed f6 first: after f6 gxf6, Black's own pawn on f6 blocks the e7–h4 diagonal, and only then is Qh4 strong. Played immediately, Qh4 landed right on the e7-queen's diagonal — Qxh4 won your queen on the spot.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (860) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: h4 sits on the open e7-h4 diagonal — f6 and g5 are empty — and no white piece covers it, so Qh4 is met by Qxh4, winning your queen outright. Already four pawns down, that ended any realistic hope; the f6 push afterward was a gesture. Qa3 kept the game alive: from a3 the queen hits the c5 knight through b4, and after Re8 c4 d4 g4 the position stays sharp enough to hunt for a swindle.

drills.explanation: Your e5 pawn was attacked by the d6 pawn; exd6 resolves it cleanly, and after cxd6 you develop normally with Nc3 and Nf3, dead level. Pushing e6 instead placed the pawn on a square Black's f7 pawn covers: fxe6 ate it for free, and after Bd3 Nf6 c4 you were simply a pawn down out of the opening.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (860) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: After d6 hit your e5 pawn, exd6 keeps the extra pawn cleanly: cxd6, then Nc3 and Nf3 develop against a passive setup and you stay +2.5. e6 instead pushes the pawn onto a square f7 guards: fxe6 wins it for nothing, opens Black's f-file, and gifts him a healthy d6-e6 pawn pair. One push forward turned your best asset into a donation.

drills.explanation: Your queen on e5 was attacked by the knight on f3, and Qe6 was the safe square — it stays active and out of reach, and if Nd4 chases it again, Qe5 returns with White having nothing better than repeating. Qd6 stepped onto the open d-file instead: Rad1 hit the queen with your own rook on d8 lined up behind it, and after Qb4, Rxd8+ Bxd8 handed White the file and the initiative.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (859) — Polish Opening
drills.coachNote: White's f3 knight was hitting your e5 queen, and d6 is the one flight square standing on the file White seizes next: Rad1 came with tempo, Qb4 was forced, and Rxd8+ Bxd8 left the a7 knight marooned on the rim and your bishop passive on d8. Qe6 held the balance — Nd4 hits it, Qe5 returns, Nf3 repeats, and a level game stays level. You scraped the draw anyway, but the hard way.

drills.explanation: Bxb4 just takes a free pawn — the whole point of testing the Polish. White regains it with Bxe5, but after Nf6, c3, Be7 you've swapped his wing pawn for your e-pawn and developed smoothly. Nc6 guarded e5 but walked into b5: the knight had to retreat to e7, and Bxe5 took the pawn anyway — White kept his extra pawn and gained time.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (859) — Polish Opening
drills.coachNote: The b4 pawn was hanging: Bxb4 just takes it, and if Bxe5 you have Nf6, c3 Be7, and after d4 you're a clean pawn up in a normal position. Nc6 defends e5 but walks into the Polish's one trick — b5! boots the defender, and after Nce7 Bxe5 the b2 bishop has eaten your center pawn and rules the long diagonal. Move two, and White's opening had already paid off in full.

drills.explanation: Nd4 does everything at once: it saves the knight from the d5 pawn and attacks White's queen on f3, winning a free tempo. After Qd1 you follow with Bf5 and b5, developing with real energy. Nce7 retreated to a passive square that blocks your own f8 bishop, and gave White time for d4, building a big centre unchallenged.
drills.title: vs Aldr_in (958) — Kings Pawn Opening
drills.coachNote: Your c6 knight was hit by the d5 pawn, but it had a forward answer: Nd4! attacks the f3 queen, and after Qd1 you follow with Bf5 and then b5 against the c4 bishop — the initiative flips to you. Nce7 retreats into your own furniture: it blocks the f8 bishop and locks your king in the center, and White simply built the big center with d4, met Qd6 with Ne2, and stayed comfortably better.

drills.explanation: Kb6 steps to safety, and play stays balanced: Rh5 Rg8, then Rxf5 for him and Rxg3 for you — an even pawn-grab. Kd6 walked onto exactly the square the knight on e5 forks from f7: Nf7+ hits king and rook together, and Ke7 Nxd8 Kxd8 costs you a rook for a knight. From e5, f7 was the springboard covering both d6 and d8.
drills.title: vs Aldr_in (958) — Kings Pawn Opening
drills.coachNote: The rook check along the seventh forced a king move, and White's e5 knight decided which squares were safe: from f7 it forks d6 and d8. Kd6 stepped straight into it — Nf7+ won the d8 rook (Ke7 Nxd8 Kxd8), and the endgame was done. Kb6 was out of every fork's reach, and after Rh5 Rg8 Rxf5 you get Rxg3: pawns come off, your g2 bishop stays strong, and real drawing chances remain.

drills.explanation: The e5 pawn was attacking your bishop on f4, and Nxe5 turns the threat around: it wins the pawn, and after Nxe5 Bxe5 your bishop sits beautifully in the center eyeing g7, leaving Black scrambling with Qg6 before you castle and meet Bf5 calmly. Castling first simply dropped the bishop — exf4 took it for free, and you were a piece down by move nine.
drills.title: vs dangalindo97 (884) — London System
drills.coachNote: e5 was attacked three times — the d4 pawn, f3 knight and f4 bishop — against only the c6 knight, and your bishop was hanging to exf4 the whole time. Nxe5 wins the pawn and solves everything: Nxe5 Nxe5 Bxe5, and after Qg6 you castle with a healthy extra pawn. Castling first let exf4 take the bishop clean — nothing recaptures on f4 — and after c4 Nf6 Re1+ you were a piece down in the game you went on to lose.

drills.explanation: Qg4 stepped straight onto the c8-bishop's open diagonal — c8 to g4 had nothing in the way, and Bxg4 took the queen; after h3, Be6 the bishop went home and Black was simply up material. Qh5 was the safe square, or better yet Nd2 first: develop the stranded b1 knight, tuck the queen on e1, and Nf3 finally challenges the bishop on g5.
drills.title: vs dangalindo97 (884) — London System
drills.coachNote: Black's c8 bishop has an open road to g4 — d7, e6 and f5 are all empty — so Qg4 hangs the queen: Bxg4, and a bad position became a dead one; this sealed the loss. Qh5 made the same threat safely: it attacks the g5 bishop from a square nothing touches while eyeing f7, and after Be7 Nd2 Be6 you win back a pawn with Rxf4 and keep fighting.

drills.explanation: Both Black bishops were loose — f5 to your bishop on d3, g5 to your knight — but capture order decided everything. Bxf5 takes the one with a counter-capture available, since the f5 bishop and your d3 bishop attack each other; after gxf5, Nd4 centralizes powerfully. Nxg5 allowed the in-between move Bxd3, and after Qxd3 Qxg5 Black had won the piece right back.
drills.title: vs LeoAlayon (759) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Both black bishops came at you, but the one on f5 was the dangerous one — it attacks your d3 bishop, so capture order was forced. Nxg5 allows the in-between Bxd3: after Qxd3 Qxg5 material is level, but your best light-squared piece is gone and Black's queen sits actively on g5; even f4 doesn't fix it. Bxf5 first removes the attacker: gxf5 wrecks Black's kingside, and Nd4 follows, hitting c6 and f5 with h4 coming.

drills.explanation: Bxf5 resolves the bishop standoff your way: after Bxg5 Nxg5 Qxg5 you have Bd7 — the bishop lands in Black's camp hitting the knight on c6 and the rook on e8. dxe5 handed Black the move order: Bxg5 came first, and after Bxf5 gxf5 Nd4 the trades fizzled — about a pawn and a half lost purely to capturing in the wrong sequence.
drills.title: vs LeoAlayon (759) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Same standoff a move earlier: your g5 bishop faces f6, Black's f5 bishop eyes d3, and the captures had to come in the right order. dxe5 lets Black choose — Bxg5 snapped your dark-squared bishop first, and after Bxf5 gxf5 Nd4 the dust settles with you worse; the e5 pawn is a weakness, not a trophy. Bxf5 was correct: after Bxg5 Nxg5 Qxg5, your bishop lands on d7, forking the c6 knight and the e8 rook.

drills.explanation: Your queen on a8 had the bishop on c8 pinned against the queen on d8, and Rfc1 — either rook works, but the f1 rook keeps a1 at home — piles onto it down the fully open c-file. Black's best is to bail out with Be6, when Qxd8+ Nxd8 trades into a pleasant endgame for you. g3 let the moment slip: d5 and then Nd6 covered c8, and the target wriggled free.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs hansk74 (917) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: At +6 the target was the c8 bishop your a8 queen was already leaning on. g3 spent a tempo on nothing, and d5 let Black breathe — the knight reroutes to d6 and you gave back two pawns of pressure, though the win survived. Rfc1 piles a second piece toward c8: after Be6 you trade with Qxd8+ Nxd8 and push d5 yourself, hitting the bishop that just moved.

drills.explanation: Nxe4 wins the e4 pawn cleanly — d5 can't recapture, because that would open the b3–g8 diagonal from your queen straight onto Black's king. After f5 the knight slides back to g3, and c4 starts prying at d5 next. Nh2 retreated for nothing: f5 cemented Black's pawn on e4, and you had to play f3 to challenge it anyway, a tempo and a pawn poorer.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs hansk74 (917) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: The e4 pawn was simply free: its only defender is the d5 pawn, and with f7 gone d5 is pinned to the g8 king on your queen's b3–g8 diagonal — dxe4 is literally illegal. Nh2 retreated from a pawn you could take, and f5 immediately cemented e4, handing Black kingside space and the e-file after Re8. Nxe4 wins it clean; when f5 comes, the knight drops to g3 and c4 starts dissolving Black's center.

drills.explanation: White's queen on e2 stares down the e-file at your king on e8 — Kf8 steps out of that pin, and after White castles, Re8 takes the open file with b5 coming next. Be7 planted the bishop directly into the pin: White castled long, and the moment you castled kingside the bishop on e7 lost its defender — Qxe7 won it clean.
drills.title: vs timis511 (783) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: White's queen on e2 owns the e-file, and your king on e8 was the only thing making e7 look safe. Be7 walks into that stare: after O-O-O you castled, and Qxe7 just took the bishop — the moment your king left e8, nothing guarded it. This is the move that lost the game. Kf8 sidesteps instead, keeping the bishop working on f6; then Re8 takes the e-file yourself and b5 harasses the c4 bishop.

drills.explanation: Nd7 saves the attacked knight and, crucially, plugs the a4–e8 diagonal to your king; after exd6 you castle long, White's Qd5 gets kicked by Nb6, and Qf5+ is only a check. dxe5 ripped open the d-file with your king still on e8: Bb5+! and the cheapest escape was Qxb5 Nxb5 — queen for bishop. The capture looked free; the open lines behind it weren't.
drills.title: vs timis511 (783) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: You were already struggling, and the live wire was b5: your king on e8 and queen on a5 both sit in its blast radius, while e5 attacks your f6 knight. dxe5 grabs the pawn and gets hit by Bb5+ — after Qxb5 Nxb5 your queen is gone for a bishop. Nd7 was the defense: it saves the knight from the e5 pawn, and after exd6 you castle long into a grim but fightable position.

drills.explanation: Bxc3+ wins a clean piece: the bishop takes the knight with check, the king steps back to c1, and Qd4 then dominates — after Rd1, Qc5 keeps the extra material with a raging attack. Qxb3+ gave away the queen for far too little: the c2 pawn simply captured it, and even after Rxb3+ Kc2 Rxc3+ you had only a knight and two pawns for her. The knight on c3 was the loose piece; the b3 pawn was defended.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.title: vs iwanpagun (934) — Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.coachNote: The b3 pawn is guarded by the pawn on c2 — Qxb3+ isn't a blow, it's an unintended queen sacrifice. cxb3 takes her, and though Rxb3+ Kc2 Rxc3+ scoops back a knight and pawns, you're fighting a queen with a rook; this move lost the game. Bxc3+ was the right capture: the f6 bishop takes the knight with check on the b2 king, and after Kc1 Qd4 your queen and rook converge while White's h1 rook sleeps.

drills.explanation: The bishop on g5 was hanging to your h6 pawn — hxg5 takes it, and White gets nothing back: after g4, Nh7 keeps the extra piece, and Nd5 is answered by Bxd5 Rxd5. Qb7 gave White the tempo to bail out with Bxf6, and once your e7 bishop recaptured on f6, the d6 pawn lost its guard — Rxd6 followed at once.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.title: vs iwanpagun (934) — Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.coachNote: Your h6 pawn was already attacking the g5 bishop — hxg5 wins a piece for a pawn, and White's best is the desperate g4 storm, when Nh7 tucks the knight away and you stay a bishop up. Qb7 ignored a piece that was both hanging and dangerous: Bxf6 removed your knight, Bxf6 recaptured, and Rxd6 grabbed the pawn while hitting your e6 bishop. From clearly better to lost ground in one move, in a game that slipped away.

drills.explanation: hxg5 wins a pawn and creates a passed pawn of your own: after Rd8, Rh1 drops your rook to the back rank where it holds up Black's e-pawn — e2 is met calmly — and gxh6 sets your runner loose. Kc2 was too passive: Rg8 and Kd4 activated Black's rook and king together, his connected threats growing while your rook sat tied to h5.
drills.title: vs eddyfrias (879) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: You're worse, but your h5 rook was biting on g5 — hxg5 now, and after Rd8 Rh1 e2 gxh6 your own h-pawn runs, with real swindle chances. Kc2 handed Black the tempo for Rg8, and once the rook defended g5 the chance was gone: the king marched to d4, the e-pawn ready to roll, and the losing course was set.

drills.explanation: hxg5 first — dissolve Black's kingside pawns while your king alone handles the passer: e2 is met by Kd2, then Re8, Ke1 and the e-pawn is stopped dead. Kd1 allowed g4 instead: the g-pawn became a passed runner with the rook coming to g8 behind it, while your own rook drifted from f5 back to f1, purely defensive.
drills.title: vs eddyfrias (879) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: One move later hxg5 was still the saving idea — after e2 Kd2 you corral the pawn while gxh6 creates your own runner. Kd1 neither stops the e-pawn nor starts counterplay: g4! and the pawn simply sprints, and after Rf5 Rg8 Rf1 you're passive while the g- and e-pawns come down the board together. Two chances at the same resource, both missed.

drills.explanation: White's bishop on e4 was attacking your rook on a8, and Bb5 answers with a counter-threat against the rook on f1 — so after Bxa8, Bxf1 and the damage cancels out. Qxc5 grabbed a pawn but ignored the corner: Bxa8 took the rook, and White's bishop slid back along the long diagonal to f3 in time to cover f1.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs LeJuAl (890) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: The e4 bishop's diagonal runs unobstructed through d5, c6 and b7 straight to your a8 rook — that x-ray was the whole position. Qxc5 grabbed a pawn while the rook hung: Bxa8 took it, and though Bb5 hitting the f1 rook clawed material back (Bf3 Bxf1), you'd sold the exchange for a pawn. Bb5 played first is the counter-threat: if Bxa8 then Bxf1, and the mutual captures leave you only slightly worse.

drills.explanation: bxc5 wins a clean pawn: the d4 pawn daren't recapture because it's the lone defender of the knight on e5, so White retreats with Qc2 and you keep the extra pawn. Nxe5 released the tension backwards — dxe5 hit your f6 knight, and after Ne4 Nxe4 dxe4 everything traded off with White's structure intact and the c5 pawn still cramping your queenside.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs LeJuAl (890) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: The e5 knight leaned entirely on the d4 pawn, so bxc5 wins material: if dxc5 in reply, Nxe5 is free. Your Nxe5 was the same idea in the wrong order — dxe5 recaptured and kicked the f6 knight, and after Ne4 Nxe4 dxe4 your e4 pawn sits on the b1-bishop's h7 diagonal, a lasting target. After bxc5 Qc2, g6 blunts that battery and your rooks take the b-file.

drills.explanation: After White grabbed your e5 pawn, the queen gets it straight back — Qd4 forks the knight on e5 and the pawn on e4, so Nf3 saves the knight but Qxe4+ regains the pawn with check, and the queen trade leaves a level endgame (Qe7, hitting the knight the same way, also works). Qf6 attacked from the wrong angle: d4 simply propped up the knight, and you stayed a pawn down.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.title: vs justme_abhi (895) — Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.coachNote: Down the e5 pawn, the queen had to regain it with a threat that lasts. Qf6 attacks the e5 knight for exactly one move: d4 defends it while grabbing the center, and after Bd6 Nc4 your queen got shooed to g6 with the pawn never recovered. Qe7 is the mechanism — it hits e5 down the e-file, and when the knight retreats with Nf3, Qxe4+ takes back the pawn with check and trades into an endgame where you keep the bishop pair.

drills.explanation: Your queen on f6 was attacked by the knight on g4 — Qh4 saves her with interest: the knight is hit twice (queen plus the c8 bishop) and defended only by the queen on d1, so it retreats to e3, and Qxe4 collects the central pawn for free. Bxg4 saved the queen by donating your bishop — Qxg4 recaptured, and after h5, Qf3 White stood fine.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.title: vs justme_abhi (895) — Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.coachNote: You're a pawn down and the g4 knight just attacked your queen — the job is regaining e4, not trading your only developed piece. Bxg4 gave White everything: Qxg4 develops the queen for free, and after h5 Qf3 White keeps the extra pawn and the better game. Qh4 does two jobs at once — steps off the attack while hitting the g4 knight — and after Ne3 Qxe4 the pawn comes straight back.

drills.explanation: Bxc3 wins the knight and — crucially — keeps your queen on d6 plugging the d-file, so Qa8+ can be met by Kd7, and even after Rxd6+ Kxd6 Qxb7 you're still fighting. Qb6 attacked White's queen but stepped off d6, handing the d-file to the rook on d1 — d7 was no longer an escape square, and Qa8 was checkmate on the spot.
drills.title: vs paam66 (1011) — Grob Opening
drills.coachNote: Your queen on d6 was doing invisible work: it blocked the d1 rook's file, which kept d7 as your king's escape from the white queen camped on a7. Qb6 dropped the shield — Qa8 is mate on the spot: d7 is covered by the rook, c7 is your own pawn, and the h8 rook can't reach b8 past your king. Bxc3 first grabs the knight, and after Qa8+ Kd7 works precisely because your queen still guards the d-file; Rxd6+ Kxd6 Qxb7 leaves a real fight.

drills.explanation: Qh4 swings the queen out with Bf4 to follow, attacking White's queen — in the line he must bail with Qxd4, and after Rxd4 Rxd4 you've given rook and knight for the queen, the stronger army here. Nf5 abandoned d4 and unblocked the e3–a7 diagonal: Qa7! dove in beside your king, and after c6, Nc5 piled on, forcing Bb8 just to evict the queen.
drills.title: vs paam66 (1011) — Grob Opening
drills.coachNote: You were winning: the d4 knight and e5 bishop had White's queen and king in a vice. Nf5 pokes the queen but releases the grip — Qa7! slid down the freed e3–a7 diagonal to sit beside your king, and with Nc5 coming at b7 you were reduced to c6 and Bb8; the +4 evaporated. Qh4 keeps the octopus on d4 and attacks: after Kb1 Bf4 the queen is cornered, and Qxd4 Rxd4 wins queen for knight.

drills.explanation: Rdc1 doubles rooks on the open c-file with the brutal threat of Rc8+ — after Qd7, only then is Qxh4 safe, and when Black grabs d5, Rc8+ drags the king out to e7. Taking on h4 immediately ran into Nf3+: the knight forked your king and queen, and after Rxf3 the queen on e7 recaptured on h4 straight through the g5 square the knight had just vacated.
drills.title: vs ShoreZee (944) — Caro Kann Defense Classical Variation
drills.coachNote: The h4 pawn was a trap: Nf3+ forks your g1 king and the h4 square, and the jump is also a discovery — once the knight leaves g5, the e7 queen sees h4 down the diagonal. Qxh4 fell into both: Nf3+, and after Rxf3, Qxh4 took your queen for a knight — from +2.4, this is the move that lost the game. Rdc1 first: the Rc8+ threat pulls the queen to d7 off that diagonal, and then Qxh4 is safe, meeting Rxd5 with Rc8+.

drills.explanation: Your g2 pawn was pinned — the rook on g6 x-rays it straight to your king — so h3 was really defended only by the rook on f3. Rxf7 removed that defender: Qxh3 crashed in, and Qxg2+ followed with the g6 rook backing the queen up. g4 keeps the queen shut out instead, and the calm Rdd3 and Rd1 regrouping holds; Black wins back only the d5 pawn.
drills.title: vs ShoreZee (944) — Caro Kann Defense Classical Variation
drills.coachNote: Already lost, but your f3 rook was the last defender — it held h3 and kept the third rank closed against the h4 queen. Rxf7 abandoned the post: Qxh3 crashed in, and after Kf1 Qxg2+ Ke2 the king gets hunted with nothing around it. g4 was the sternest try, forcing Black to find Rxg4+ hxg4 Qxg4+ before Rg3 — still losing after Qxd1+, but making Black earn it rather than collapsing at once.

drills.explanation: The bishop on b5 is giving check, and c6 deals with it perfectly: it blocks and attacks the bishop in one move. After Bxc6+ bxc6 Nxe4 cxd5, the dust settles with you collecting White's d5 pawn — a healthy result. Ke7 also escaped the check but did nothing about your bishop hanging on e4: the knight on c3 took it for free with Nxe4.
drills.title: vs VitaminB5 (926) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: Your e4 bishop was attacked by the c3 knight with no defender anywhere — that fact was the whole move. Ke7 did nothing about it, and Nxe4 just won the piece. c6 was the saving resource: the pawn forks the b5 bishop and the d5 pawn, so after Bxc6+ bxc6 Nxe4 cxd5 the captures cancel and you're only somewhat worse instead of a piece down. You won anyway, but this should have ended it.

drills.explanation: Bf6 reroutes the bishop, and the tactics then work for you: when Rf2 hits your bishop on c2, Bxf5! grabs the pawn as it escapes, and after Bxf5 gxf5 you're a pawn up with only Rg3+ to weather. gxf5 won the same pawn but left c2 stranded: Rc1 hunted it down, and Be4 Nxe4 fxe4 left you a weak e4 pawn with White's bishop entrenched on e6.
drills.title: vs VitaminB5 (926) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: Your bishop on c2 is stranded deep in White's camp — that piece, not the f5 pawn, is what the position turns on. gxf5 opened your king and gave White Rc1: the bishop's only out is Be4, and Nxe4 fxe4 leaves your structure shredded with the e6 bishop still lodged next to your king. Bf6 first repositions the h4 bishop, and then Bxf5 — the c2 bishop escapes via d3 and e4 while collecting the very pawn you grabbed, same capture, right piece.

drills.explanation: Your queen was parked on d8 — the pawn's own promotion square. Qf6 fixes that: it keeps d8 covered along the f6–d8 diagonal and defuses Black's one trick, since Nf3+ gxf3 Rxf3 runs into Qxf3, scooping the rook (Qf8 works too). The immediate d7 left the pawn blocked by its own queen, and Nf3+ then Ne5 hit the pawn while your king was dragged around.
drills.title: vs Micheldrx (913) — Scandinavian Defense
drills.coachNote: At +8.5 Black's only prayer was the Nf3+ trick from the d2 knight, backed by the e3 rook. d7 ignored it: Nf3+ Kf2 Ne5 and the knight swings back at your new d7 pawn, while Kxe3 walks into the counterattack — twelve pawns of eval gone in one push. Qf6 defuses the trick before pushing anything: after Nf3+ gxf3 Rxf3, Qxf3 scoops the rook because the queen guards f3 along the file. You still won, but this turned a clean kill into a scramble.

drills.explanation: Send the queen hunting with Qc8: she gets behind the black king and herds it — Kg6, Qe8, Kg5 — while the d6 passer waits to run. Bc2+ handed Black a free block with g6, and the initiative flipped: after d7, Nf3+ dragged your king to f2 with the knight and the e3 rook suddenly swarming. The check looked active but bought nothing.
drills.title: vs Micheldrx (913) — Scandinavian Defense
drills.coachNote: A check isn't progress: g6 blocks Bc2+ cleanly and your bishop then stares at a wall. The real jobs — escorting the d6 pawn and defusing Black's Nf3+ ideas — went unattended, and after g6 d7 Nf3+ Kf2 the knight danced into your camp with a chunk of your advantage. Qc8 was the coordinating move: the queen comes back to shepherd the passer, and after Nc4 Qxc4 f5, d7 walks the pawn home with the queen at its side.

drills.explanation: Nd7 tucks the knight under your king on c7 and frees the rook: Rf8 next hits White's queen on f7, and even after Qxg7 your king slides to d6 with everything still messy. Kb8 abandoned d7 — White swung a rook to b1 against the b7 pawn in front of your king, and when the knight came to d7 anyway it was unguarded: Qxd7 took it.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs Basha_z (860) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Your queen on c2 was off raiding while your king's shelter depended on the b-file staying shut. Kb8 stepped into Rfb1 — everything trains on b7, your queen is too far away to help, and the defense Nd7 got smashed by Qxd7; a level game became minus eight on the spot. Nd7 played immediately was the difference: the knight guards the entry squares, Rf8 comes with tempo on the f7 queen, and even after Qxg7 your king walks out to d6 and survives.

drills.explanation: d2! decides the game: the pawn attacks the rook on e1, and if Qxc2 grabs your queen, dxe1=Q+ promotes with check — queens traded, a full rook won, and your new queen follows up with Qe5+. c4 wasted the passed pawn's one moment and ignored his runner: a6!, and after Qc3, axb7+ Ka7 left your king bare beside the white queen on g6.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs Basha_z (860) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: The race came down to one tempo: d2 hits the e1 rook, and if Qxc2 then dxe1=Q+ — you promote with check, which decides everything. c4 pushed the wrong pawn, and a6! flipped the board: axb7+ was coming, and with Qc3 you were suddenly defending a mating net around your a8 king instead of queening. In a mutual race, the line that ends in check wins the argument — and d2 had it.

drills.explanation: axb4 wins the b4 pawn outright — the knight on c3 doesn't cover it — and throws open the a-file: after Ne2+ Kh2 the rook storms in with Ra7, leaning on the bishop on b7 and the c7 pawn. Rae1 let Black capture first with bxa3, and that a3 pawn became a dangerous passer with Nb5 hopping back to escort it while your rooks watched.
drills.opening: Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.title: vs bbaldy87 (491) — Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.coachNote: Black's b4 pawn was attacking a3 with the a8 rook parked behind the whole file — that tension was the position. Rae1 let bxa3 through, and the passer became a monster: with the c3 knight covering the promotion path, your f3 and Rf2 were pure containment while Nb5 escorted it down. axb4 removes the problem at the root, and after Ne2+ Kh2 Rh8 you answer the activity with Ra7, your rook landing on the seventh.

drills.explanation: Black's a2 pawn was one square from queening, and your f1 rook — the only piece covering a1 — was outgunned because the a8 rook backs the pawn. Ra1 plugs the queening square permanently; Rfe1 then keeps the other rook active against Nd5 and the king. Re5 ignored it: a1=Q, Rxa1, Rxa1+ — a whole rook gone for that pawn.
drills.opening: Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.title: vs bbaldy87 (491) — Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.coachNote: One glance at a2 decides every candidate move: the pawn is a square from queening with the a8 rook behind it. Re5 doesn't address it — a1=Q forced Rxa1 Rxa1+, and you're a full rook down. Ra1 was the only move: park the e1 rook on the promotion square itself; after Nd5 Rfe1 you're still worse, but the pawn is dead-stopped and the game goes on. You escaped with the win, but this deserved to lose.

drills.explanation: Your knight on b6 had a whole rook hanging on c8 — Nxc8 simply takes it, and after Kd8 the knight escapes through d6; Black's Qd2+ is a one-off check that Kf1 ends. f4 hit the queen but loosened everything around your king on e2: Qxg3 grabbed a pawn, and after Rhg1 the queen took f4 too — two pawns gone and the rook on c8 untouched.
drills.opening: Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.title: vs bbaldy87 (472) — Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.coachNote: Your b6 knight was already latched onto the c8 rook — Nxc8 wins the exchange on the spot. f4 pokes the queen but pulls f2's protection off g3: Qxg3 grabbed the loose pawn with threats at g2 and f4 both, and after Rhg1 Qxf4 your e2 king was the one under fire — from +3.4 to lost in one push, saved only by your opponent's level.

drills.explanation: a4 keeps things tidy and gains queenside space; the d4 knight gets challenged properly later with Ne2, when Nxf3+ is just a trade. Nxd4 stumbled into the recapture: exd4 forks your bishop on e3 and knight on c3. The in-between tries — Qh5+ Kd8, then Nb5 hitting his queen — rescue the knight, but the bishop falls: a piece for a pawn.
drills.opening: Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.title: vs bbaldy87 (472) — Kings Pawn Opening Kings Knight Variation
drills.coachNote: Black's e5 pawn was loaded: any capture on d4 gets answered by exd4, forking your c3 knight and e3 bishop in one move. Nxd4 stepped on it — exd4 hit both pieces, and the bail-out Qh5+ Kd8 Nb5 chases the king and queen without recovering the material. a4 was the mature choice: leave d4 alone, gain space with a5 against b6, and challenge the knight later with Ne2 when it costs nothing.

drills.explanation: The knight on a5 was staring at your c4 pawn, and Qd3 covers it while keeping the queen centralized — after Qe7 you regroup calmly with Bd2 and Rd1, and the queenside holds (Raf1 was also fine). Qh5 went hunting on the wrong wing: Nxc4 took the pawn and hit your bishop on e3 at the same time, and after Bf2, Qxb5 collected a second pawn.
drills.title: vs Gitto11 (1026) — Giuoco Piano Game
drills.coachNote: The a5 knight had exactly one target — your c4 pawn — and your queen on e2 was its only defender. Qh5 chased an attack that doesn't exist (the f7 rook and g8 king cover everything) and abandoned c4: Nxc4 hit, Bf2 Qxb5 saw a second pawn fall, and Rh4 was you flailing rather than attacking. Raf1 was consistent: double against f6 while the queen stays guarding c4, and after Qe7 you probe with h4, everything still defended.

drills.explanation: Qe2 retreats with purpose: it adds a defender to the bare c4 pawn (the a5 knight was eyeing it) and clears the way for Bd2, putting the question to that rim knight. Rf5 abandoned e4 — your f4 rook was its only guard — so Rxe4 took it for free, and after the Rf3 retreat, Nxc4 grabbed a second pawn anyway. Two loose pawns, one careless rook move.
drills.title: vs Gitto11 (1026) — Giuoco Piano Game
drills.coachNote: Black's Re5 attacked your h5 queen, and your f4 rook was the only piece holding e4. Rf5 blocks the attack but quits that job: Rxe4 just took the pawn, and after Rf3 Nxc4, c4 followed while your queen stayed stranded offside. Qe2 does everything in one retreat — steps out of the rook's attack, keeps Rf4 guarding e4, and re-covers c4 against the a5 knight. Worse but solid, with Bd2 rerouting next.
